structure.notes: The skeleton sections are present and the checklist is actionable, but the locked-parameter block declares a PartialID support-function anchor while the run metadata declares the exactid cluster; the main theorem statements in Section 9 are also too compressed to carry the displayed formulas and exceptional branches needed downstream.

novelty.notes: The derived kernel is a generic support-face contact identity once eta_w^*(P) is defined as the first radius at which the LP maximizer set intersects Q_eta(P). It does not establish a flagship-level equivalence or non-equivalence frontier between named published estimators or identification strategies.

correctness.notes: The basic compactness argument for the frontier is largely sound, but some claimed strengthening is underproved, especially the nonempty open semi-algebraic class and the two-component O(2) reduction as stated in the theorem rather than only in the proof.

perItemFindings.label: Formal setup / locked parameters
perItemFindings.one_line: The note's locked block declares a PartialID support-function anchor even though the run metadata and Lean target identify the cluster as exactid, creating anchor drift for later stages.

perItemFindings.label: Theorem 1: critical leakage support frontier
perItemFindings.one_line: The equivalence h_LP=h_eta iff eta >= eta_w^*(P) follows almost immediately from defining eta_w^*(P) as the first leakage level at which the LP support face intersects Q_eta(P), so the current derivation is below the enforced flagship floor.

perItemFindings.label: Related work and positioning
perItemFindings.one_line: The note cites LP, sign-SVAR, and non-Gaussian SVAR papers, but it does not name a specific published estimator or workflow whose equivalence frontier is newly characterized in the required flagship sense.

perItemFindings.label: Main theorems
perItemFindings.one_line: The Section 9 theorem statements are conclusory confirmations and do not themselves expose the formulas, infeasibility branches, or quantified hypotheses that are later used in Section 11.

perItemFindings.label: Proof of Conjecture 1
perItemFindings.one_line: The proof of a positive finite frontier on a nonempty open semi-algebraic class relies on a single constructed center and informal perturbation claims rather than a complete certificate that the advertised observable class is nonempty and open under all stated restrictions.

perItemFindings.label: Proof of Conjecture 2
perItemFindings.one_line: The global minimum over rotation and reflection components is asserted using an angular relabelling and norm-preservation argument that is plausible in two dimensions but not stated with enough hypotheses in the theorem itself.

verbatim_critique:
The artifact is structurally close to a reviewable Stage 0 note, with the required sections, labelled assumptions, support-function definitions, sanity checks, counterexample/minimality checks, and a useful math-object checklist. The first problem is anchor drift. The brief places this run in the exactid cluster, but the locked block in Formal setup declares a PartialID support-function structural time-series anchor, and the later counterexample section explicitly says the shared setup is PartialID despite the exact-ID run metadata. Because the note is in fact written as a PartialID support-function theorem, later exactid-specific stages would not know whether to formalize do-calculus-style identification or algebraic support bounds.

The main mathematical derivation is not flagship-level. The displayed critical radius is defined as the first eta for which the exposed LP maximizer set intersects the eta-contact set. With nested closed contact sets, compactness, and support attainment, the equality h_LP=h_eta iff that intersection is nonempty is a standard support-function observation. The proof of Conjecture 1 then repackages this definition as the headline frontier. That can be a useful organizing lemma, but it is not a new equivalence frontier between named published estimators or identification strategies, nor does it resolve an open non-Gaussian SVAR identification regime. The cited literature is relevant, but the comparison remains generic: LP/sign relaxation versus higher-cumulant contact set. It does not identify two named estimators or workflows and prove a new sharp threshold separating their equivalence and non-equivalence regimes.

The theorem statements in Main theorems need to carry the actual content. Theorem 1 says the conjecture is confirmed and mentions positivity and finiteness, but the operative definition, feasibility convention, and nonempty-contact qualification are outside the statement. Theorem 2 is only a prose sentence, while the formula with the plus-infinity branch and componentwise minimization appears earlier and in the proof. For downstream use, the statement itself should expose the component formula, the pure-diagonal feasibility branch, and the global aggregation over O(2).

On correctness, the core compactness argument for the equality frontier is mostly recoverable. The weakest part is the advertised nonempty open semi-algebraic class. The proof gives one two-shock center and says perturbations preserve the required inequalities and regular isolated-boundary condition after removing an exceptional set. That is not yet a complete certificate that the claimed class of observable VAR/cumulant objects is nonempty, open, and semi-algebraic under the stated population restrictions. The two-shock proof also needs a sharper statement of the hypotheses under which reflection only relabels or sign-changes off-diagonal fourth-cumulant coordinates while preserving the residual norm and the LP support value.

A repair aimed at the current theorem would likely produce a sound subfield or field-level support-function lemma. To meet the enforced flagship target, the note would need a genuinely stronger kernel: for example, a threshold theorem tied to named published LP and SVAR identification procedures, with an if-and-only-if frontier that is not merely induced by the definition of the critical radius, or a generic-class result showing a new open regime for higher-cumulant SVAR support identification.
